metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the exact post-rollback behavior when old `resign_plan_review` encounters a new-format `plan_review_result_v2` sidecar carrying `review_phase`/`priority_floor`: either (a) specify that re-sign must be disabled/blocked during the rollback window, or (b) assert that the old code's silent-ignore behavior is safe (explain why) and add a rollback-window AC that prohibits re-sign operations until forward upgrade, analogous to the compaction-pause instruction.
evidence: Plan keeps schema identifier as `plan_review_result_v2` (no version bump) so old readers accept the new-format sidecar as valid v2
Plan states old resign_plan_review 'retains its existing sidecar verdict and own-material-fingerprint guards' — no phase parsing — meaning old code proceeds without phase validation
Rollback section says 'existing phase-bearing snapshots remain lossless' but makes no claim about sidecar-driven re-sign correctness
SIGNATURE events are immutable (I1 invariant documented in concurrency.md); a bad re-sign cannot be undone
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the post-rollback schema state of `plan_review_result_v2` sidecars written by new code (containing `review_phase`/`priority_floor`) when consumed by rolled-back old `resign_plan_review`. Old resign code has no `parse_review_phase_metadata` call; it reads the sidecar's own-material and verdict fields but silently ignores the new phase fields, and could produce a re-signed attestation with a phase that new code would have rejected as incompatible. Since SIGNATURE events are immutable in the append-only log, such a re-sign is irreversible. DATA LOSS RISK: phase enforcement guarantee is permanently lost for any ticket re-signed by old code during the rollback window.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Persist review-time phase data' / 'resign_plan_review calls this named parser' + Rollback section in docs/plan-review-gate.md
scenarios: Operator rolls back after deployment; a developer triggers failed-sign recovery (re-sign) through old code; old code reads the new-format sidecar, ignores review_phase, and produces a signed execution-phase attestation from a planning-phase review (or vice versa); the signature is now permanently committed to the append-only log

After forward upgrade, compute_validity reads the immutable SIGNATURE manifest and finds phase=planning while compiled state=execution; the attestation is permanently incompatible with no recovery path
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the rollback section: 'During the rollback window, re-sign operations are prohibited in addition to compact; an attempt by old code to re-sign a new-format sidecar must be blocked at the operator level (gate via the same enforcement-disable step that disables claim/close). Document that old code reading a new-format sidecar will ignore review_phase and may produce phase-incompatible signatures.' Alternatively, bump the sidecar schema to `plan_review_result_v3` so old readers skip the new sidecar entirely (returning legacy-absent behavior) and cannot inadvertently re-sign from it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `pytest -q` line for the rollback test file (e.g. `tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py::TestRollback` or a dedicated `tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_rollback.py`) in the Verification section so the AC has a concrete, named proving command.
evidence: The five explicit `pytest -q` commands in Verification cover reducer, manifest, validity, floor, resign, sign-transaction, interfaces, contracts, and docs — but no file path for the rollback scenario is listed.
'Run `make check` and the full non-LLM test suite, including the named integration and rollback tests' is prose, not a proving command with a file path; the rollback test file name is never stated.
findings.finding: The rollback AC ('The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status, with the documented precision-loss window') has no corresponding named pytest command in the Verification section — it appears only in the catch-all 'make check … including the named integration and rollback tests' prose, not as an explicit `-q` invocation with a file path.
findings.location: Verification section; AC: 'The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status, with the documented precision-loss window.'
scenarios: A reviewer or CI operator cannot confirm that the rollback AC is exercised without inferring which test file contains the rollback scenario; if the rollback test is omitted from the implementation the catch-all make check line would not surface the gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the schema/code contradiction for legacy persisted precheck outputs: either (a) use an expand-contract sequence (first deploy `review_phase` as optional in the closed schema, then promote to required), or (b) explicitly document and test that schema validation is skipped/loosened for legacy persisted payloads with a schema-version discriminator.
evidence: Plan states precheck output schema is closed (additionalProperties:false) and adds review_phase to required array
Plan also states 'absence in a legacy persisted run defaults planning' — implying the field can be absent in persisted payloads
These two statements are contradictory: schema requires the field, but code must accept its absence from pre-upgrade persisted runs
No expand-contract (optional → required across two versions) or schema-version routing is described for the persisted case
findings.finding: The closed `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` (additionalProperties:false) is updated to require `review_phase`, but the verify-failure recovery path explicitly reads `precheck["review_phase"]` from a *persisted* precheck payload that was serialized before the upgrade — such a payload will lack the field. The code handles absence by defaulting to planning, but the schema says `required`, so schema validation of a legacy persisted precheck output will fail. No expand-contract sequence (first add as optional, then in a later version promote to required) or validator bypass for legacy payloads is described.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'The verify-failure recovery in workflow/gate_dispatch.py reads precheck["review_phase"] and passes the same bool; absence in a legacy persisted run defaults planning' + 'update the already-closed plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json properties/required arrays while retaining additionalProperties: false'
scenarios: An operator upgrades mid-flight while a plan-review is in verify-failure retry state; the persisted precheck output lacks review_phase; schema validation rejects the payload; recovery is broken
Rollback and re-upgrade cycle: old precheck outputs remain on disk and must be re-consumed by the new code with the strict closed schema
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `schema_version` integer field (or use a versioned schema file pair) so the recovery reader selects the pre-upgrade schema for old payloads and the new schema for new ones. Alternatively, do a two-phase rollout: Phase 1 makes `review_phase` optional with a default in the closed schema; Phase 2 (next release) promotes it to required once all persisted payloads have been migrated.

evidence: 36 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 9 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a detection and recovery procedure for tickets re-compacted by old code during the rollback window before compact was paused, analogous to the `creation_channel` I9a pattern in `docs/concurrency.md` (identify affected ticket IDs from the compaction commit range; specify whether `rebar fsck --repair-snapshots` or an equivalent rebuild recovers `plan_review_phase` from raw events, or whether the precision loss is accepted as permanent with a documented bound).
evidence: Plan states: 'operators accept that documented loss of historical phase precision during the rollback window'
docs/concurrency.md I9a provides the precedent pattern: stop compact, capture affected IDs from compaction commit range, recover with `rebar fsck --repair-snapshots`
Plan provides no equivalent fsck detection flag or repair path for `plan_review_phase`-bearing SNAPSHOTs re-compacted by old code
findings.finding: The rollback section does not specify a recovery procedure for tickets that were compacted during the upgrade window before the operator paused `rebar compact`. Unlike the existing `creation_channel` rollback pattern in `docs/concurrency.md` (I9a), which provides an fsck-based detection step (`SNAPSHOT_STALE_CHANNEL`) and a `rebar fsck --repair-snapshots` recovery path, this plan has no equivalent: it says operators 'accept documented loss of historical phase precision during the rollback window' but provides no concrete mechanism to identify affected tickets or recover them after forward upgrade.
findings.location: Implementation section — Rollback paragraph: 'If old code fully re-reduces raw events after its own cache bump, the phase field is absent and a later forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status'
scenarios: Operator pauses compact too late; 50 tickets were re-compacted by old code during the window; their SNAPSHOTs now lack plan_review_phase; after forward upgrade these are silently re-bootstrapped from compiled status, but bootstrap from status is lossy (e.g. a ticket in `in_progress` that was reviewed in planning phase gets misclassified as execution)
There is no way to distinguish tickets whose phase was bootstrap-recovered from their true historical phase vs tickets whose phase was accurately bootstrapped

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a feature flag (e.g., `PLAN_REVIEW_EXECUTION_FLOOR_ENABLED`) gating the 0.80-floor Pass-3 enforcement and phase_status blocking, specify how the rollback disables enforcement (flag toggle vs. code deploy), and add an AC proving the flag off-path restores pre-feature Pass-3 behavior without data migration.
evidence: '(max(block_threshold, 0.80), blocking_enabled) only when execution_review and blocking_enabled' — activates immediately on deploy for all execution-phase reviews
'Rollback disables claim/close enforcement first and pauses every `rebar compact` invocation for the downgrade window' — mechanism for disabling enforcement is unspecified
Rollback AC tests only the data precision-loss path (field drop and forward reboot), not the enforcement-off toggle
No feature flag, percentage rollout, or canary path is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-floor enforcement and phase_status compatibility checking activate as a single 100%-traffic flip on deploy — there is no feature flag, canary gate, or staged-rollout path described. Any new execution-phase review immediately faces the blocking floor, and the rollback procedure ('disable claim/close enforcement first') is described only in prose without specifying the mechanism (config flag, code revert, emergency override), leaving it untested as a quick toggle.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Define orchestrator.pass3_over_findings' / § 'Rollback disables claim/close enforcement first' / AC: 'The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction...'
scenarios: An operator deploys the feature and discovers the 0.80 floor is rejecting legitimate reviews; rapid rollback requires a code deployment or undocumented config change rather than a flag toggle.
A staged rollout to a subset of tickets/projects is impossible without a flag, so operators cannot validate behavior on low-risk tickets before full exposure.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a runtime-readable config flag checked inside `pass3_over_findings` before applying the floor lift: `if execution_review and blocking_enabled and flags.is_enabled('plan_review_execution_floor'):`. Document the flag as the rollback lever in `docs/plan-review-gate.md` and add an AC that toggling the flag off restores planning-identical Pass-3 output. This satisfies 'cheap, tested way to undo quickly' without requiring a re-deploy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC stale-detection criterion terminology with `PlanReviewGeneration` field names: replace 'relationship' and 'related-fingerprint' with the exact field names (`relation_snapshot`, `ticket_store_revision`, or sub-fields thereof) so the criterion is unambiguously verifiable.
evidence: AC text: 'Any stable phase, own-material, relationship, related-fingerprint, or generation-field change returns terminal unsigned stale.'

Implementation text: 'PlanReviewGeneration is {phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision} where relation_snapshot is child acaf's exact PlanRelationSnapshot and equality is dataclass equality across every field.'
ABSENCE: 'related-fingerprint' appears nowhere in the Implementation section's definition of PlanReviewGeneration or PlanRelationSnapshot.
findings.finding: The AC for terminal unsigned stale uses the terms 'relationship' and 'related-fingerprint' which do not map to any named field in `PlanReviewGeneration` as defined in the Implementation section. `PlanReviewGeneration` is defined as `{phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision}`. An implementer reading the AC cannot determine whether 'related-fingerprint' maps to `relation_snapshot`, a sub-field inside `PlanRelationSnapshot`, or something else entirely, and whether 'relationship' is the same as `relation_snapshot` or `ticket_store_revision`.
findings.location: AC: 'Any stable phase, own-material, relationship, related-fingerprint, or generation-field change returns terminal unsigned stale.' / Implementation: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' section
scenarios: Implementer writes tests for 'related-fingerprint' change detection and targets the wrong field, leaving a gap in stale-detection coverage.
Reviewer cannot verify the AC is satisfied without guessing the field mapping.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC line to: 'Any stable change to phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, or ticket_store_revision (all fields of PlanReviewGeneration) returns terminal unsigned stale (`plan_review_generation_changed`).' If `relation_snapshot` itself contains a fingerprint sub-field that is the meaningful equality unit, name that sub-field explicitly here and in the PlanReviewGeneration definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `PlanReviewGeneration` has ≥3 independent call sites, or simplify to a local `namedtuple`/`TypedDict` within the sign-transaction module rather than a named exported dataclass.
evidence: Plan: 'PlanReviewGeneration is {phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision} where … equality is dataclass equality across every field'

Plan: 'Full review collects the initial generation before the LLM; drift refresh and re-sign collect it immediately before their non-LLM probe' — all three are the same transactional boundary, not independent consumers
Search result: zero hits for 'PlanReviewGeneration' in src/ or tests/ (only found in .rebar/cache prior-review findings noting the same issue)
Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 independent call sites before introducing a named abstraction; only one code path (the sign transaction) is described
findings.finding: `PlanReviewGeneration` is a new named dataclass introduced with a single described production call site (the pre-sign transaction path). The plan states 'Full review collects the initial generation before the LLM; drift refresh and re-sign collect it immediately before their non-LLM probe' — but all three are the same sign-transaction flow, not three independent consumers. Grep confirms no existing `PlanReviewGeneration` symbol anywhere in the codebase. The dataclass's sole behavioral purpose is to support equality comparison across five fields; a local namedtuple or a plain `dict` with documented keys would serve equally well without the named-abstraction tax.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' paragraph; AC lines 'A stable generation equal to the initial generation signs and commits under one lock'
scenarios: An implementer introduces a named dataclass that is only ever constructed in one place, adding import surface, documentation burden, and a future migration cost for no callsite-sharing benefit.
If the sign-transaction semantics change, the dataclass schema must be updated even though it is never used outside this one path.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `PlanReviewGeneration` with a module-private `_SignGeneration = namedtuple('_SignGeneration', ['phase', 'priority_floor', 'own_material', 'relation_snapshot', 'ticket_store_revision'])` inside the signing module; do not export it. Promote to a named public dataclass only when a second independent consumer is identified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a single named module-level constant (e.g. `EXECUTION_PRIORITY_FLOOR: float = 0.80`) in `manifest.py` or `attest.py` and reference it in all three locations: the manifest builder, the Pass-3 closure, and the sidecar v2 validator.
evidence: Plan manifest section: 'execution manifests contain exactly review-phase: execution then priority-floor: 0.80'
Plan Pass-3 section: '_threshold_for closure … returns (max(block_threshold, 0.80), blocking_enabled) only when execution_review and blocking_enabled'
Plan sidecar section: 'execution writes JSON number priority_floor: 0.8 (not string and not null)'
Existing pattern: sidecar.py defines RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY = 0.5 and RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50 as named module-level constants; orchestrator.py defines DEFAULT_ADVISORY_CAP = 20
Plan explicitly states: 'Raising it later invalidates lower-floor execution attestations' — a named constant is the standard mitigation for this kind of cross-component policy value
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-floor constant is scattered across at least three separate components with no named shared constant: (1) the manifest builder emits the literal string `priority-floor: 0.80`, (2) the Pass-3 closure computes `max(block_threshold, 0.80)`, and (3) the sidecar v2 JSON serializes `priority_floor: 0.8`. The plan explicitly documents that changing this value has protocol-wide consequences ('Raising it later invalidates lower-floor execution attestations; lowering it accepts higher-floor attestations without migration'), which is precisely the condition under which a named constant is warranted. The existing codebase uses named constants for similar policy values (e.g. `RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY = 0.5`, `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50`, `DEFAULT_ADVISORY_CAP = 20`).
findings.location: Implementation sections: manifest builder ('execution manifests contain exactly priority-floor: 0.80'), Pass-3 closure ('max(block_threshold, 0.80)'), sidecar v2 schema ('execution writes JSON number priority_floor: 0.8')
scenarios: A future operator raising the floor updates the Pass-3 constant but misses the manifest builder or sidecar validator, causing a silent cross-component inconsistency where manifests record 0.80 but enforcement applies 0.90.
A test author hardcodes 0.80 in three different test files, making the constant's role invisible and future changes require hunting down all occurrences.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `EXECUTION_PRIORITY_FLOOR: float = 0.80` to `manifest.py` (alongside the other manifest-layer constants like `_MANIFEST_PREFIX`). Import and use it in `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings`'s threshold closure and in `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata`'s validation logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete fix in the implementation bullet: e.g. 'if the field is in the strip-list, remove it; if absent from an explicit keep-list, add it — then add an AC test that asserts the field survives a compact round-trip.'
evidence: 'Verify `_snapshot_strip_keys()` in `_commands/compact.py` does not strip it' — this is a 'check if' instruction with no stated action for the failure case

The corresponding AC ('Normal SNAPSHOT compaction and older-reducer snapshot pass-through preserve the exact field value') requires a correct outcome but the implementation path to get there when the current code strips the field is unspecified
findings.finding: The Implementation instruction 'Verify `_snapshot_strip_keys()` in `_commands/compact.py` does not strip it' is a 'check if' placeholder that defers to the executor what to do when the check fails — if the function currently strips the field, the plan gives no design decision for how to fix it (remove from strip-list, add to keep-list, or otherwise).
findings.location: Implementation section, first bullet: 'Verify `_snapshot_strip_keys()` in `_commands/compact.py` does not strip it.'
scenarios: Executor finds that `_snapshot_strip_keys()` currently includes `plan_review_phase` in its strip-list and must decide independently whether to remove it from the list, rename it, or use an allowlist pattern — a real design choice left open.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Verify `_snapshot_strip_keys()` in `_commands/compact.py` does not strip it' with 'Confirm `_snapshot_strip_keys()` does not include `plan_review_phase` in its strip set; if it does, remove it from that set (or add it to the explicit keep-list if the function uses one).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC lines or Verification annotations that explicitly distinguish the scope of `tests/unit/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py` (e.g., unit-level threshold closure behavior) from `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py` (e.g., end-to-end workflow YAML branch blocking), so implementers know exactly what each file must contain.
evidence: Verification line 2: 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_validity.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py'

Verification line 4: 'pytest -q tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_sign_transaction.py tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py tests/interfaces/contracts/test_workflow_schema_golden.py'

AC text: 'A production workflow integration test starts from compiled execution phase and proves both YAML decide branches receive review_phase and block a finding below 0.80 that planning would not block.' — maps to the interfaces file only, leaving the unit file's scope undeclared in AC.
findings.finding: The Verification section lists `test_plan_review_execution_floor.py` at two distinct paths — `tests/unit/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py` (second pytest command) and `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py` (fourth pytest command) — without distinguishing what each file is responsible for. No AC explicitly names or differentiates these two files' scopes, making it unclear whether they are intentionally separate files covering unit vs. integration aspects, or whether one path is a copy-paste error.
findings.location: Verification section: second pytest command (`tests/unit/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py`) and fourth pytest command (`tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py`)
scenarios: Implementer creates only one file and places it at one of the two paths, omitting the other level entirely.
Implementer creates two files with overlapping coverage and no clear boundary, causing maintenance confusion.
findings.suggested_fix: Annotate the Verification section: 'tests/unit/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py — unit: threshold closure returns floored pair when execution+blocking, original pair otherwise; tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_execution_floor.py — integration: full workflow from compiled execution phase through both YAML decide branches, verifying a sub-0.80 finding blocks.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named `plan_review_result_v2.schema.json` artifact (or equivalent canonical schema file) and reference it in both `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata` and the `docs/plan-review-gate.md` documentation sections.
evidence: plan_review_result_v2 schema is described only in prose within the Implementation section
The five required docs/plan-review-gate.md sections listed do not include the sidecar v2 schema contract
No .schema.json file path is named for the sidecar format
sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata is specified but has no schema artifact to validate against or reference
findings.finding: The `plan_review_result_v2` sidecar schema is fully specified in prose (key names, types, null/bool/string rejection rules, malformed combinations) but no corresponding `.schema.json` artifact, schema file path, or doc section covering it is named. This persistence format governs failed-sign recovery and re-sign correctness, yet its machine-readable contract has no canonical location and is not referenced in `docs/plan-review-gate.md`'s five required sections.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery with an exact additive plan_review_result_v2 schema' + 'Document the phase/floor manifest contract, health compatibility table, fixed-floor rationale, generation transaction, and rollback behavior in docs/plan-review-gate.md'
scenarios: A future implementer adds a new sidecar field and doesn't realize it is constrained by the v2 schema, introducing a silent malformed combination
Consumers of the sidecar (re-sign, audit) diverge in their interpretation of the prose spec without a canonical machine-readable schema

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `drift_refresh_candidate` (attest.py:146-179) is updated (or that 75de explicitly wires it through `compute_validity`) so that phase enforcement is inherited, rather than relying on the false premise that it already shares `compute_validity`.
evidence: Plan text: 'After prerequisite 75de is implemented on the feature branch, claim, idempotent reuse, and drift refresh share compute_validity(DEFAULT|DRIFT_REFRESH) and inherit phase enforcement.'
attest.py:146-179: drift_refresh_candidate calls signing.verify_signature, manifest_regver, manifest_material, manifest_deps, _rehash — no compute_validity call anywhere in the function.
attest.py:728-766: claim_gate_check calls compute_validity (line 759) — confirmed.
__init__.py:454: _idempotent_reuse calls claim_gate_check which calls compute_validity — confirmed.
ABSENCE: grep for 'compute_validity' in drift_refresh_candidate body returns no results.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Claim, idempotent reuse, and drift refresh already share compute_validity(DEFAULT|DRIFT_REFRESH) and inherit phase enforcement' as a scope-exclusion premise, but `drift_refresh_candidate` (attest.py:146-179) does NOT call `compute_validity` — it has its own inline staleness checks (verify_signature, manifest_regver, manifest_material, manifest_deps, _rehash). Phase enforcement will NOT automatically flow to the drift-refresh path.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'Extend 75de's compute_validity plan-review branch...' — 'Claim, idempotent reuse, and drift refresh share compute_validity(DEFAULT|DRIFT_REFRESH) and inherit phase enforcement.'
scenarios: After this ticket ships, a drift-refresh re-sign runs on a ticket in execution phase — the phase enforcement gate in compute_validity is bypassed entirely because drift_refresh_candidate never calls it, allowing an incompatible or out-of-date phase attestation to be refreshed without phase validation.

The AC 'Claim, reuse, drift refresh, and re-sign enforce phase through the shared validity/phase helpers' cannot be satisfied by 75de alone if drift_refresh_candidate does not route through compute_validity.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a `compute_validity(DEFAULT)` call inside `drift_refresh_candidate` (after 75de ships) before returning a candidate, or (b) explicitly name `drift_refresh_candidate` as an edit target in the Implementation section with a note that it must delegate to `compute_validity` to inherit phase enforcement — do not rely on the current inline-check path to inherit it automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `ManifestFormatError` is defined by acaf in `manifest.py` and re-exported from `attest.py`; this plan's `manifest.validate_review_phase_metadata` raises the existing class rather than defining a new one.
evidence: Plan text: 'The helper raises `ManifestFormatError` for duplicates signaled by callers, missing/extra tokens, unknown phase, bool/non-numeric/non-finite floor, or floor outside `[0.0, 1.0]`'
acaf ticket (already-signed prerequisite): 'Define ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() in manifest.py; attest.py imports and re-exports both beside the existing manifest helpers.'
acaf AC: 'Every malformed pin variant raises ManifestFormatError from manifest.py.'

ABSENCE: the plan under review never references acaf as the source of ManifestFormatError, never says 'import from manifest.py (defined by acaf)', and never states whether this plan extends or reuses acaf's class.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `ManifestFormatError` as if this plan defines it (e.g., 'The helper raises `ManifestFormatError` for...', 'Every named phase/floor syntax error raises `ManifestFormatError`'), but prerequisite ticket acaf (already reviewed and passed) already specifies that `ManifestFormatError` belongs to `manifest.py` and is re-exported from `attest.py`. If this plan independently defines `ManifestFormatError` in `manifest.py`, it creates a duplicate definition that conflicts with acaf's ownership. If it relies on acaf's definition, the plan never says so — leaving unverified whether the class will exist at the expected import path and who owns its interface.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Restructure `build_manifest`'s fixed initial list...' paragraph; also AC 'Every named phase/floor syntax error raises `ManifestFormatError`'
scenarios: If acaf ships ManifestFormatError with a specific interface (e.g., message format, subclass hierarchy) and this plan defines a parallel ManifestFormatError with different semantics, callers that catch ManifestFormatError by name will catch the wrong type in edge cases.
If acaf is not yet implemented when this plan's tests run, the missing ManifestFormatError will cause import errors that obscure the real test failures.
findings.suggested_fix: In the implementation section, replace 'The helper raises `ManifestFormatError`' with 'The helper raises `ManifestFormatError` (defined by acaf in `manifest.py`, re-exported from `attest.py`)' and add acaf as an explicit dependency in the deps list if not already present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence architectural rationale in the `PlanReviewGeneration` section explaining why the write lock cannot span the LLM/probe call (long-running operation) and therefore why the optimistic-concurrency retry is required rather than a simpler lock-held approach.
evidence: Plan text: 'Retry outside the lock at most three times only for an unstable tracker revision: read clean tracker HEAD, collect a fresh generation, read HEAD again; unequal HEADs emit structured WARN `plan_review_generation_retry` with attempt/before/after and retry.'
Plan text: 'Under the global write lock, require the same clean HEAD and exact generation, then append and commit the signature atomically.'

ABSENCE: no statement of why the write lock cannot be held from collection through signing (e.g., 'LLM calls are long-running and holding the lock would block concurrent operations'), and no mention of considered alternatives such as a single snapshot-collection-under-lock step.

Existing codebase pattern: the write lock is already used only for append+commit (the LLM call occurs outside it), confirming the lock cannot span the LLM call — but this constraint is never stated in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke three-attempt optimistic-concurrency retry transaction (`PlanReviewGeneration` + HEAD-stability retry loop + under-lock re-verification) without stating why a simpler design — extending the existing write-lock scope to cover the snapshot collection step — was considered and rejected. This is a novel architectural pattern within this codebase with meaningful complexity (retry logging, exhaustion returns, mismatch detection) that warrants a brief justification.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction...' and 'Before signing, retain one immutable `initial_generation`...' paragraphs
scenarios: A reviewer or future maintainer may attempt to simplify by extending lock scope without realizing the LLM call's duration makes that approach infeasible.

The retry logic (3 attempts, WARN logging, exhaustion behavior) adds substantial complexity; if the architectural constraint driving it is not documented, a future refactor might remove it, re-introducing the race condition.

findings.finding: `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` has `"additionalProperties": true` (it is an OPEN schema), but the plan's Implementation section and AC call it a 'closed schema' and plan to 'update their properties and required arrays atomically' — closing it and adding `review_phase` must be done in one step, but the plan treats it as already-closed which is incorrect.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add required `review_phase` with string enum `planning|execution` to both closed schemas `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`' / AC: 'Closed-schema golden tests prove `review_phase` is required in both declared schemas'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, change 'both closed schemas' to 'both output schemas (plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json, which is already closed, and plan_review_decide_input.schema.json, which must be closed atomically)'. Add an explicit AC: 'plan_review_decide_input.schema.json has additionalProperties: false after this change is applied'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test file (e.g. `tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_rollback.py`) covering old-code re-reduction dropping the unknown field and forward-upgrade bootstrap from compiled status, and include it in the explicit `pytest -q` invocation in the Verification section.
evidence: No pytest invocation in the Verification section names a rollback test file (e.g. test_plan_review_phase_rollback.py)
'make check and the full non-LLM test suite, including the named integration and rollback tests' — 'named rollback tests' are not actually named
AC exists but has no corresponding file in the five explicit pytest commands
findings.finding: The rollback scenario — old-code raw-event re-reduction silently drops the unknown `plan_review_phase` field, and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status with documented precision loss — is required by an AC and referenced in 'make check … including the rollback tests', but no named pytest file appears in the explicit `-q` pytest invocations in the Verification section. This leaves the rollback behavior untethered from the automated suite and risks the AC closing green without the path ever being exercised.
findings.location: Verification section (pytest commands) and AC: 'The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status, with the documented precision-loss window.'
scenarios: A reviewer runs only the listed pytest commands, all pass, and signs off — but the rollback precision-loss path was never run
An operator downgrades the reducer after phase-bearing snapshots accumulate; the undocumented data loss is never caught by CI
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_rollback.py` to the first pytest invocation line, and write tests that: (1) build a snapshot with `plan_review_phase` present, replay it through a reducer that does not know the field, assert the field is absent; (2) then run the post-upgrade reducer and assert bootstrap fires and emits `plan_review_phase_bootstrap` INFO.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion explicitly verifying that bootstrap raises the existing reducer validation error (not a guess) when compiled status is unknown, and add a corresponding test assertion in `test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py`.
evidence: Implementation text: 'An unknown status raises the existing reducer validation error rather than guessing.'
ABSENCE: No AC line reads 'Bootstrap raises the existing reducer validation error for an unknown compiled status' or similar.
Related AC lines cover all other bootstrap behaviors but omit this branch: 'Pre-feature open/idea snapshots bootstrap planning once; every other known status bootstraps execution once.'
findings.finding: The bootstrap behavior for an unknown compiled status — 'An unknown status raises the existing reducer validation error rather than guessing' — is described in the Implementation section but has no corresponding acceptance criterion. Every other bootstrap behavior (open/idea → planning, other known → execution, cache versioning, INFO emission, no-ticket-event) has a dedicated AC, but this error path is unverified.
findings.location: Implementation: 'Pre-feature open/idea snapshots bootstrap planning...' section / AC section (absent)
scenarios: A future refactor accidentally swallows the unknown-status error and defaults to one of the known phases, silently losing data integrity without a failing test.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC line: 'Bootstrap raises the existing reducer validation error and emits no phase when compiled status is unknown (not open, idea, or any other recognized status).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a structured log event (e.g. WARN `plan_review_sign_lock_mismatch` with attempt/generation_before/generation_after fields) for under-lock generation or HEAD mismatch retries, matching the observability level of the pre-lock `plan_review_generation_retry` WARN.
evidence: 'emit structured WARN plan_review_generation_retry with attempt/before/after and retry' — this WARN is scoped to the pre-lock HEAD-instability retry loop only
'Under-lock mismatch consumes a retry' — no log/metric emission specified for this path
Retry budget is shared between both paths but observability is only specified for one
findings.finding: Under-lock generation/HEAD mismatch retries are silent: the plan specifies structured WARN `plan_review_generation_retry` only for the pre-lock HEAD-instability path, but under-lock mismatches that consume the same retry budget emit no log event. Operators cannot distinguish under-lock contention exhaustion from pre-lock instability exhaustion in observability tooling.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' and 'Before signing, retain one immutable initial_generation' sections; AC line 'Under-lock mismatch retries; LockTimeout returns retryable unsigned; neither writes a signature.'
scenarios: Retry budget exhausted entirely by under-lock mismatches returns 'retryable unsigned' with no WARN emitted, leaving operators unable to diagnose why signing is repeatedly failing without tracing code paths manually.
Mixed pre-lock and under-lock retries each partially consuming the budget produce ambiguous or missing structured log evidence.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the under-lock retry path: emit structured WARN `plan_review_sign_lock_mismatch` with fields `{attempt, reason: head_mismatch|generation_mismatch, ticket_id}` each time an under-lock check fails and a retry is consumed. This mirrors the pre-lock WARN pattern already specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and a corresponding test case in `test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py` that asserts bootstrapping a snapshot with an unknown/unrecognized compiled status raises the expected reducer validation error.
evidence: ACs for bootstrap only specify: 'Pre-feature open/idea snapshots bootstrap planning once; every other known status bootstraps execution once'
'An unknown status raises the existing reducer validation error rather than guessing' — absent from AC list

test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py is the only named test file for bootstrap, but its scope description ('lifecycle projection, bootstrap, compaction, grammar, ordering, and parser compatibility') does not mention unknown-status error
findings.finding: The Implementation section specifies that bootstrapping an unknown compiled status 'raises the existing reducer validation error rather than guessing', but no AC and no named test file covers this failure path — making it an untested branch in the new bootstrap logic.
findings.location: Implementation section, bootstrap paragraph: 'An unknown status raises the existing reducer validation error rather than guessing.' No corresponding AC exists; no test file is named.
scenarios: A snapshot with a corrupted or future status value is bootstrapped; the error path is silently wrong (wrong exception type, wrong message, or swallowed) and only discovered in production
A future status enum addition is tested for execution bootstrap but silently falls through to the unknown-status error path
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC list: 'An unknown compiled status at bootstrap raises the existing reducer validation error.' Add a parametrized test case in test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py with a synthetic unknown status value (e.g. 'future_status') and assert the specific exception class is raised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `execution_review: bool` flag on `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` with a caller-supplied `threshold_for` override closure at the two call sites, matching the pattern already used in `code_review/workflow_ops.py:419`.
evidence: Grep: orchestrator.pass3_over_findings called at workflow_ops.py:484 and gate_dispatch.py:375 — exactly 2 call sites
Plan: 'Its local _threshold_for closure lexically captures the parameter' — the parameter is only used to wrap the closure, not shared across callers
Existing pattern: code_review/workflow_ops.py:419 passes a different threshold_for directly at the call site rather than adding a boolean flag to the shared kernel function
Rule-of-Three: ≥3 independent call sites required before parameterizing an existing public function with a new behavioral flag
findings.finding: `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` is proposed to receive a new `execution_review: bool = False` keyword argument, but grep confirms exactly **two** production call sites: `workflow_ops.plan_review_decide` (line 484) and `gate_dispatch.py` (line 375). Adding a boolean flag parameter to a function that is only called from two places to change its internal closure behavior is a Rule-of-Three violation — the flag is premature at the `orchestrator` level. The behavior could instead be expressed by passing a different `threshold_for` closure directly at those two call sites, which is already the established pattern in the codebase (`code_review/workflow_ops.py` line 419 passes `threshold_for=registry.threshold_for` directly).
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Define orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs, *, execution_review: bool = False)' paragraph; AC 'Execution Pass-3 destructures (block_threshold, blocking_enabled) and floors only the float threshold when blocking is enabled'
scenarios: A future caller wants a different floor (e.g. 0.90 for a stricter policy) and discovers the boolean only supports 0.80, requiring another parameter or a flag enum — flag proliferation.
The two existing call sites differ in how they obtain the phase value (one from compiled state, one from a persisted precheck), making the boolean's semantics fragile across contexts.
findings.suggested_fix: At each of the two call sites, construct `threshold_for = _execution_threshold_for if execution_review else _threshold_for` locally, then pass it to `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(rest, rest_verifs, threshold_for=threshold_for)`. Define `_execution_threshold_for` as a module-level helper in `orchestrator.py` that wraps `_criteria.threshold_for` with the 0.80 floor. This keeps `pass3_over_findings`'s signature stable and the floor logic caller-visible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `SidecarReviewPhaseError` has ≥3 independent catch sites, or remove it and have `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata` either re-raise `ManifestFormatError` directly or return a typed result object that `resign_plan_review` inspects without catching a named exception.
evidence: Grep: zero matches for 'SidecarReviewPhaseError' anywhere in src/ or tests/ (only found in .rebar/cache prior-review findings)
sidecar.py: all existing error handling uses broad Exception catches with logger.warning and never defines domain-specific exception classes
Plan: 'resign_plan_review calls this named parser, compares the result with current compiled phase, and refuses incompatible/malformed re-sign' — only one consumer named
Rule-of-Three: a named exception type is justified when ≥3 independent catch sites benefit from type-specific handling; here there is one catch site (resign_plan_review)
findings.finding: `SidecarReviewPhaseError` is introduced as a new bespoke exception class with exactly one described consumer (`resign_plan_review`) and zero existing analogues in `sidecar.py`. Looking at the existing sidecar error handling pattern — all `sidecar.py` functions catch `Exception` broadly and return `None`/`False` with `logger.warning` — the codebase explicitly avoids bespoke sidecar exception types. The plan's own description ('translates `ManifestFormatError` to `SidecarReviewPhaseError`') creates an error-type translation layer for a single catch site, which is the classic premature-abstraction pattern.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Add sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata(payload) -> ReviewPhaseMetadata; it passes raw JSON values to manifest.validate_review_phase_metadata and translates ManifestFormatError to SidecarReviewPhaseError'
scenarios: An implementer introduces a new exception class and import, registers it in the module's public surface, and writes tests for it — all for a single catch site that could equally handle the underlying ManifestFormatError directly.
The translation layer (ManifestFormatError → SidecarReviewPhaseError) obscures the original error context without adding actionable information.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` as an explicit edit target for the `process_status` phase-projection rule and the `process_snapshot` pre-feature bootstrap (mirroring the existing managed_refs/attestations/keyring migration pattern at lines 681–720).
evidence: process_status defined at src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:212 — handles STATUS events including fork detection and winning/losing branch logic

process_snapshot defined at src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:662 — the exact function that performs pre-feature bootstrap migrations (per the existing managed_refs/attestations/keyring migration pattern)
Plan names only `reducer/_cache.py` explicitly among reducer files
ABSENCE: neither _processors.py nor any other reducer module beyond _cache.py is named as an edit target
findings.finding: `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` is an unenumerated edit target: both `process_status` (which must gain the `plan_review_phase` projection rule for winning/losing STATUS events) and `process_snapshot` (which must gain the pre-feature bootstrap logic) reside in this file, but it is never named in the Implementation section or file_impact.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Add reducer-maintained `plan_review_phase`…' and 'For a pre-feature snapshot without the field, bootstrap…'
scenarios: Implementer modifying only reducer/_cache.py would miss the STATUS-event phase projection and snapshot bootstrap entirely
The bootstrap migration pattern (lines 681-720 of _processors.py) is exactly where the plan's pre-feature snapshot bootstrap must live, mirroring managed_refs/attestations/keyring migrations
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation's file edit list: `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` — extend `process_status` to set `state['plan_review_phase']` on winning STATUS entries; extend `process_snapshot` with a pre-feature bootstrap guard analogous to the existing `managed_refs`/`attestations`/`keyring` migrations.

findings.location: Implementation section — 'workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck emits the compiled value' and 'plan_review_decide requires the field and calls orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(…, execution_review=(review_phase == "execution"))'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate existing `build_manifest` call-site test files (`test_attestation_signing.py`, `test_plan_review.py`, `test_gate_engine_cutover.py`, `test_plan_review_convergence.py`, `test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py`) as requiring review/update for backward-compatibility after the manifest restructure, or confirm the new params are keyword-only with planning defaults.
evidence: tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py:293 calls attest.build_manifest({"verdict": "PASS", "ticket_id": "t"}, material="m")
tests/unit/test_plan_review.py:657 calls attest.build_manifest(verdict, material="deadbeef")
tests/unit/test_gate_engine_cutover.py:47-54 calls attest.build_manifest(v, material="fp-abc", deps=…, regver=…)
tests/unit/test_plan_review_convergence.py:137 calls attest.build_manifest(…)
tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py:28 calls attest.build_manifest(…)
None of these test files are named as edit targets; plan only names the new golden/cutover tests
findings.finding: Existing `build_manifest` callers in test files (`tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py`, `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py`, `tests/unit/test_gate_engine_cutover.py`, `tests/unit/test_plan_review_convergence.py`, `tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py`) are not enumerated as requiring updates despite the plan restructuring `build_manifest`'s fixed initial list to conditionally emit `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` lines — their manifest output will silently default to planning-phase format if new params use defaults, or break if params are required.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Restructure `build_manifest`'s fixed initial list to emit exact lines in this order after `advisory:` and before `rebar-version:`'
scenarios: If build_manifest gains required keyword-only phase/floor params (no default), all five existing test files would fail at call time
If defaults yield planning-phase manifests, tests pass but may yield stale golden values if any test asserts on manifest line order/content
The plan says 'Update schema golden/cutover tests' but does not enumerate the five existing test files that directly call build_manifest
findings.suggested_fix: Confirm that new `build_manifest` parameters (`phase`, `floor`) are keyword-only with defaults (`phase='planning'`, `floor=None`), ensuring all five existing callers continue to produce planning-phase manifests without modification; document this in the Implementation.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
